strengths: Novel claim-level schema with explicit validity intervals, provenance, polarity, and volatility
Clear empirical demonstration that the LLM linker is causal: ablating it drops every measured slice
Strong abstention behaviour (80 % explicit abstain vs 0 % for Flat RAG) while maintaining equal outcome accuracy on the full 30-question set
Efficiency win: −63.5 % tokens vs A-Mem with comparable latency
Comprehensive reproducibility package (prompts, code, seeds, raw outputs)
weaknesses: Overall accuracy statistically ties Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %, p=0.21) and is numerically lower, limiting practical upside
Routers recover Flat’s accuracy but do not exceed it; oracle upper bound (82 %) shows large unrealised complementarity
Intent classifier recall low on key slices (MS 42.5 %, KU 39.1 %), leaving substantial headroom
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS slices traced to over-abstention and sparse assistant-side claim extraction
Evaluation limited to 150-question stratified subset; full 500-question run deferred to camera-ready
required_revisions: Provide full 500-question LongMemEval-S results to confirm representativeness claims
Ablation or sensitivity analysis on linker thresholds (τ_hard, τ_sim, τ_bypass) to justify chosen values
Error analysis with confidence intervals for router per-slice performance to quantify uncertainty
Clarify whether the 80 % abstention rate on the 30-question set holds under reader temperature ablation
Release exact counts of hard-edge density and Δ distribution to substantiate “greedy is optimal” claim

strengths: Novel claim-level schema with validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges
Explicit abstention policy evaluated on dedicated 30-question abstention set
Paired McNemar tests and bootstrap CIs reported for every key comparison
Full ablation of LLM linker shows causal drops on all targeted slices
Reproducibility package: code, prompts, seeds, raw per-question outputs released
Efficiency gain: –63.5% tokens vs A-Mem while adding temporal semantics
weaknesses: Only 150-question stratified subset used for primary claims; 500-question run postponed to camera-ready
SSU slice n=21 and abstention n=9 are under-powered (trend-level p-values)
No error bars or significance tests on token-cost and latency tables
Router relies on a 64.7%-accurate intent classifier; MS recall only 42.5%
No cross-dataset transfer: LongMemEval-S only; LoCoMo/MemoryAgentBench absent
Hyper-parameters (τ_hard, k_keep, similarity gates) not ablated or sensitivity-analysed
required_revisions: Run and report the full N=500 LongMemEval-S replication with CIs before camera-ready
Add paired McNemar or bootstrap tests to efficiency table to confirm token/latency differences are significant
Provide a power analysis justifying the N=150 budget and the detectable effect size
Ablate key hyper-parameters (τ_hard, k_keep, similarity thresholds) to show robustness
Evaluate on at least one external long-context benchmark (LoCoMo or MemoryAgentBench) to test generalisation
Release the exact versioned model checkpoints or API call hashes to lock the LLM behaviour for full reproducibility

strengths: Claim-level schema with validity intervals and explicit contradict/supersede edges is a clean abstraction for truth maintenance
Session-batched writer cuts token cost 63 % vs A-Mem while preserving abstention behaviour
Paired wins on abstention (80 % explicit abstain vs 0 %) and single-session-user (100 % vs 90.5 %) are concrete
Router composition recovers overall accuracy (67.3 % vs 68 % Flat) while keeping truth semantics where needed
Full prompts, seeds, and per-question outputs released—good reproducibility package
weaknesses: Overall accuracy 61.3 % is numerically 7 pp below Flat RAG; McNemar p=0.21 but under-powered n=150
Intent classifier recall 42.5 % on multi-session leaves 15 pp on the table vs oracle upper bound
Regresses on assistant & multi-session slices because claim graph misses assistant entities and cross-session aggregates
All numbers are on a 150-question subset; 500-question replication promised but not shown
Token cost still 12× Flat RAG; latency 4.7 s vs 2.9 s—non-trivial overhead for deployment
required_revisions: Run and report the full N=500 LongMemEval-S evaluation to remove subset risk
Ablation show linker is causal; add a cheaper linker ablation (e.g. rule-based or smaller LM) to justify cost
Quantify end-to-end dollars: token cost × API price to give practitioners a deployment budget
Provide latency CDF or P99; mean 4.7 s may hide tail latency that blocks real-time use
Clarify failure taxonomy fixes: denser assistant extraction and multi-claim reader composition roadmap

strengths: Novel framing of agent memory through truth maintenance lens; claim-level schema with validity intervals and contradict/supersede edges is a meaningful departure from flat RAG and note-graph approaches
Exceptionally honest statistical reporting: McNemar p-values, bootstrap CIs, Cohen's h effect sizes, and open acknowledgment that overall accuracy (61.3%) is directionally below Flat RAG (68.0%)
Well-designed ablation cleanly isolates the LLM linker's causal role; every measured slice drops when linker is removed
Thorough regression taxonomy (F1-F3) explains why TTMG loses on SSA/SSP/MS rather than leaving failures unexplained
Efficiency analysis vs A-Mem is detailed and shows genuine cost reduction from session-batched writing
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is directionally worse than Flat RAG (61.3 vs 68.0, p=0.21); the system does not beat a simple baseline on the metric that matters most in practice
Claimed wins rest on very small slices: abstention n=9 (p=0.125 two-sided), SSU n=21 (p=0.50); on the full abstention set (n=30) TTMG shows NO accuracy advantage over Flat RAG (both 76.7%)
The 'explicit abstention' behavioral difference does not translate to accuracy gains on the full abstention population, undermining the core practical motivation
TTMG is ~12x more expensive than Flat RAG in tokens per question; efficiency gains are only vs the weaker A-Mem baseline
Router compositions (67.3%, 66.0%) do not beat Flat RAG (68.0%); the oracle gap (82.0%) shows complementarity but the paper does not close it
No results on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S for TTMG; the 150-question subset may not capture all failure modes
A-Mem baseline is a reimplementation rather than the original code, raising reproducibility concerns for that comparison
required_revisions: Run TTMG on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S set to validate that the 150-question subset findings generalize; report per-slice results at full scale
Provide per-question paired outcomes for the full n=30 abstention set to demonstrate whether the behavioral abstention difference has any accuracy consequence under different reader configurations
Address the 12x token cost vs Flat RAG directly: quantify the cost-accuracy tradeoff and discuss whether the abstention/supersede semantics justify the overhead
Report confidence intervals on per-slice accuracies (not just overall) to make the small-n slice claims more interpretable
Include the original A-Mem code as an additional baseline or justify why the reimplementation is equivalent

strengths: Honest reporting: transparent about underpowered slices, non-significant p-values, and regressions
Good failure-mode taxonomy (F1-F3) explaining exactly where and why TTMG regresses vs Flat RAG
Reports effect sizes (Cohen's h) alongside p-values, acknowledging power limitations
Paired per-question analysis (McNemar) rather than just aggregate accuracy comparison
Efficiency gains over A-Mem are concrete and well-documented (-63.5% tokens)
Reproducibility commitment: code, prompts, seeds, per-question outputs, frozen model IDs
Router composition analysis with oracle upper bound is informative about complementarity
weaknesses: TTMG is numerically WORSE overall than Flat RAG (61.3% vs 68.0%) — the standalone system underperforms the baseline it claims to improve upon
Full abstention set (n=30) shows NO accuracy advantage (b=1,c=1, p=1.0), contradicting the n=9 subset finding; the 'win' is behavioral only
SSU win is 2 questions (paired 2-0, n=21, p=0.50) — not statistically significant and tiny in absolute terms
Only ONE ablation (remove linker); no ablation of claim schema alone, temporal parser, max-consistent-subgraph, or hybrid reader fallback to isolate individual contributions
A-Mem baseline is a self-reimplementation with no verification against original paper's published numbers
TTMG not evaluated on full 500-question set; only Flat RAG is, creating an asymmetry in representativeness verification
Router compositions needed to recover overall performance, but intent classifier is weak (64.7% accuracy, 42.5% MS recall) — the practical deployment story is incomplete
Regressions on SSA (94.1%→58.8%), SSP (77.8%→44.4%), MS (52.5%→45.0%) are severe and not adequately mitigated
required_revisions: Evaluate TTMG on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S set to verify subset representativeness for TTMG (not just Flat RAG)
Add ablations isolating individual components: claim schema without linker, temporal parser, max-consistent-subgraph filter, hybrid raw-turn fallback — to establish causal contribution of each
Verify A-Mem reimplementation against original paper's published benchmarks or provide per-question comparison to original A-Mem codebase
Report confidence intervals on per-slice numbers, not just overall, given the small slice sizes (n=9, n=21)
Address the full abstention set null result explicitly in the abstract and introduction — currently framed as a win but n=30 shows no accuracy advantage
Add a second strong structured-memory baseline (e.g., MemGPT) beyond the self-reimplemented A-Mem

strengths: Clean conceptual framing: truth maintenance as a first-class concern for agent memory, not just retrieval
Honest reporting of regressions and failure modes (F1-F3 taxonomy is commendable)
Ablation cleanly isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains on targeted slices
Session-batched writer is a good systems optimization vs A-Mem's per-turn loop (-63.5% tokens vs A-Mem)
Maximum-consistent subgraph retrieval is a well-formalized, deterministic procedure with approximation guarantee
Thorough statistical analysis: McNemar paired tests, Cohen's h effect sizes, bootstrap CIs
weaknesses: 12x token cost over Flat RAG (13603 vs 1100 tokens/q) with no overall accuracy gain—hard to justify in production
85s end-to-end latency per question vs 3.3s for Flat RAG—wholly impractical for real-time conversational agents; no discussion of this
Regresses on 3/6 question types (SSA, SSP, MS); these are common real-world patterns, not edge cases
Intent classifier at 64.7% accuracy with 42.5% MS recall and 39.1% KU recall is too weak for production routing
Key claims rest on tiny samples: n=9 abstention, n=21 SSU in main table; full n=30 abstention shows no accuracy advantage
No scaling analysis: how does linker cost grow with claim count? Pairwise comparison could be quadratic in long conversations
Writer entity gap (F1) and multi-session aggregation failure (F3) are fundamental to the claim-as-atom abstraction, not easily patched
No real-workload or deployment evaluation; only benchmark results on LongMemEval-S
required_revisions: Add latency analysis and discuss whether 85s/question is acceptable for target deployment; propose amortization or async ingestion strategies
Report scaling characteristics: how do linker invocations, graph size, and retrieval latency grow with conversation length (e.g., 500+ sessions)
Evaluate on the full N=500 LongMemEval-S set for TTMG, not just the 150-question subset, to confirm subset representativeness
Provide cost-effectiveness analysis: cost per correct answer or per quality-adjusted answer, comparing TTMG vs Flat RAG
Address or bound the writer entity gap quantitatively—what fraction of assistant-authored facts are missed, and can extraction density be tuned?
Discuss operational complexity: 3 separate LLM modules + intent classifier + dual index maintenance; what fails if any component degrades?
